items.question: Define null and alternative hypotheses. What does rejecting H₀ mean?
options: H₀ is proven false with certainty
Sample evidence is unlikely enough under H₀ that we abandon the default claim at our chosen α
The p-value equals P(H₀ is true)
items.answer: Sample evidence is unlikely enough under H₀ that we abandon the default claim at our chosen α
items.explanation: Rejecting H₀ is a decision under uncertainty controlled by α—not a proof. We conclude data are inconsistent with H₀ at the chosen error rate.

items.question: A one-sample t-test yields p = 0.02 with α = 0.05. What is the correct conclusion?
options: Reject H₀; there is a 2% chance H₀ is true
Fail to reject H₀ because 0.02 is close to 0.05
items.explanation: p ≤ α, so reject H₀. The p-value is not P(H₀ true); it is the tail probability under H₀.

items.question: Type I error is:
options: Rejecting H₀ when H₀ is false
Failing to reject H₀ when H₀ is true
Rejecting H₀ when H₀ is true
Failing to reject H₀ when H₀ is false
items.answer: Rejecting H₀ when H₀ is true
items.explanation: Type I = false positive (declaring an effect when none exists). Its probability is α under the Neyman–Pearson setup.

items.question: For n = 16, x̄ = 102, s = 8, testing H₀: μ = 100, what is t?
